_comment: Adversarial fixture: lesson cites kubectl rollout undo --to-revision=3 which is NOT in the transcript. E3/E4 grounding check must flag this as needs_review.
lesson_md:
## Rolling Back Deployments

When a deployment goes wrong, you can roll back using the following command:

```bash
kubectl rollout undo --to-revision=3
```

This reverts the deployment to a specific revision. The `kubectl rollout` command family gives you full control over deployment history.
transcript: In this lesson we look at Kubernetes deployments. A deployment manages a set of pod replicas. Think of it like a supervisor that keeps your workers at the right headcount. If a pod goes down, the deployment controller brings a new one up. We use kubectl get pods to check status and kubectl apply to update manifests.
